speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll one with Ivan Solti, roll one, Ivan Solti. This is roll one with Ivan Solti. Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Today's date November 15, 1995. Survivor's name is Ivan Solti. The interviewer is Carol Weisman. The city is Encino, California, USA. The language will be done in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Carol Weisman. Today is November 15th, 1995. I'm interviewing Ivan Solti in Encino, California, United States of America, for The Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Good afternoon. Would you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Ivan Solti, spelled S-O-L-T-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Solti, Ivan Andras. And this was a Hungarian name given to me a birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it spelled how?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was S-O-L-T-I was the last name. And Ivan, I-V-A-N. And A-N-D-R-A-S, for Andras.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many nicknames. [LAUGHS] But nothing you would call-- they, they used to call me Crane, because I was tall and lanky. And that's why my friends used to call me Crane, which is a Hungarian name, Golya, G-O-L-Y-A. That was just kidding, kidding that they called me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Solti, can you tell us your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is May 1st, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country were your born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born in Budapest, Hungary, which is the capital city. But I was growing up in Sznolnok, S-Z-O-L-N-O-K, a city of about 40,000 residents, which is southeast of Budapest, about 100 kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your pre-war address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we lived in a street called Grofapponyialbert-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's G-R-O-F-A-P-P-O-N-Y-I-A-L-B-E-R-T dash U, for street number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Dr. Solti, S-O-L-T-I, Elemer, E-L-E-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name Renner, R-E-N-N-E-R. First name was Maria. She used to be called Mitzi, M-I-T-Z-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your father was a doctor. Was he a medical doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a doctor of jurisprudence. He was a lawyer practicing in the city of Szolnok. And my mother was a housekeeper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what you remember about your mother and your father. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very successful lawyer, as I mentioned. He comes from a large family of five children, four boys and one girl. His parents and grandparents always lived in Szolnok. His grandfather was a religious man. He was a rabbi. His father had his own yard store, textile store. One of his brothers was a medical doctor. The other brother was a lawyer. Both of them died after World War I. And then the other, the third brother who was alive, he inherited the store. He continued the family in the store. And his sister was married in Szolnok. So his older brother and his sister both lived with their families in Szolnok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: The older brother was, his first name was Miklos, it's like Nicholas. And he had-- he was married, and he had two grown daughters, about the ages of 17, 18 at the-- in 1944. So-- and his, his sister, she was married too also. Her name was Farkas, F-A-R-K-A-S. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was her family name. What was her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name was Mariska. And she had a son, Sandor, S-A-N-D-O-R, and a daughter, Sara, S-A-R-A. And both of them survived the, the war. Both Sandor and Sara. As a matter of fact, Sara is still alive, living in Budapest right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were an only child, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had a very large extended family, as you've just said.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- my father had lots of first cousins. And everybody knew him in Szolnok, because the family lived there for generations. And of course, he was a well-known lawyer. He had a large clientele. He was a very generous person. He was elected to be the president of the Jewish elementary school's board.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the school that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the school where, where I went to. And he frequented the school many times, at which time I always got embarrassed in front of the other children, because they started teasing me when my father entered the, the classroom with a teacher or the, the principal of the school. And he was also the prosecutor of the local bar association. So if anybody got out, had anything to do which was incorrect among the lawyers, he was the person who handled that case for the bar association. [CLEARS THROAT] He loved the classical music. He loved opera. And he taught me how to get the liking for, for classical music. I remember he took me to Budapest to hear my first opera, which was Puccini's And-- [CLEARS THROAT] Not only mentally, but physically, he prepared me. He wanted me to be a good swimmer. He had me taking swim lessons. He had me taking gym classes. And he was very much for to-- for me to take ice skating lessons, so I would be a good ice skater. My mother comes from a very well-to-do, very affluent, like a land baron family. They had owned-- my mother's father, which is my maternal grandfather, he, with his brother, owned a large piece of land in the city of Komarom, K-O-M-A-R-O-M, which city was split between Slovakia and Hungary. And they owned a large land there, where they harvested wheat and tobacco and other things. They had a large staff who worked for them, and livestock, of course. And they were also grain merchants. They had a house in the city of Komarom. And they handled the business from, from this house. And my mother had her brother living there. And his, his name was Renner, R-E-N-N-E-R. His first name Imre, I-M-R-E. And he lived there with his wife and one daughter. And her name was Judit, or Judith. And my grandmother used to live with them, sometimes during the winter. And she used to live sometimes with us in Szolnok during the winter time, when there was nothing to do on the, on the ranch. And otherwise, during the summertime, she was out and supervising the, the work of the harvest. And on those occasions, I was always invited to be with her at the ranch. And, and I enjoyed that particular experience, being a city boy suddenly out in the-- in the ranch. So these were my experiences. And this was up to about 1940, '41, when we were able to freely travel from one city to the next. There was no, no problem. Actually, the city of Szolnok was a city which was, as I mentioned, 40,000 residents, and it was like a large family. The Jewish community was-- consisted of two basic groups, was the conservative group, which we called Neolog, they had their own temple. And the Orthodox, they had their own temple as well. And my parents attended services Friday and Saturday, and during the high holy days. And my father gave generously, pledging for the support of the Chevra Kadisha, and the support of the temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the Chevra Kadisha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chevra Kadisha is a burial society, which takes care of the burial arrangements for people who don't have the necessary funds to take care of it. And besides-- as I mentioned to you, besides my uncle who lived there, Miklos, and Mariska, who was my aunt, we also had another family member living in Szolnok. And he was my mother's uncle. His name was Flamm, F-L-A-M-M. His first name was Oscar. And he lived there with his wife, whom he met in Germany. The lady was German and also a Roman Catholic lady. They had no child, no children. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And his mother lived with them, who was very elderly. And she lived with them in one house. And they were the ideal couple. And they were ideal for us, because I had the benefit of both worlds, having Christmas celebrated at their house, and Hanukkah celebrated in our house. And of course, the language was strictly German. And talking about the German language, when I-- growing up-- when I have grown up, I had a nanny who came from Austria. And she-- the only conversation was in German. And so was the conversation with my relatives, in German. And this-- therefore, when we came to the ghetto and the concentration camps, I was somewhat advantageous to learn-- to know the language. My mother was educated also in Germany, and she was absolutely fluent, what they used to call Hochdeutsche. She spoke the high German language. And my father spoke fluent German as well. My mother, she, she loved her home. She loved to do baking. Every time for snacks, there was always baked goods, pastry, or something around the house. And she was fairly religious. My father was always kidding my mother that she should have been a rebbetzin, the wife of a rabbi, because she was the last one to, to leave the temple. And she had to wait until the last prayer was said or the last song was sung, and she would be closing the temple. So that was pretty much my upbringing. My father practiced law, as I said. In Szolnok there were two courts, something similar to what we have here, a municipal and a Superior Court. But in Budapest, they also had two courts, the Supreme Court, and there was like, one in between. And on many occasions, he had to travel up to Budapest to, to try cases in front of those courts. And on some of these occasions, of course, he took us along, my mother and myself. And those were very happy times, when we went to hear, like, opera, as I said, in, in Budapest, Margaret Island, there was an open-air amphitheater, where we got tickets and we heard the, the opera. And he took us to, to other theaters, not only opera, but other theaters as well. [CLEARS THROAT] And as far as friends, my friends consisted mainly of the pals I had in the elementary school. As I mentioned, it was a Jewish elementary school. And there were four classes. In other words, from first to four grade. And my friends mainly consisted of the boys and the girls who were my classmates. And I was a very studious person. I was always on the top of the list. I had to be, because as I said, my father was the head of the school board and he expected it of me. And for reward, after the finals were passed, or the certificates were given out, for rewards we went to a pastry shop, where me and some other kids were given ice cream and pies. And this was from the first year, which was in 1937, to 1940. These were the years, which were pretty happy years. And I remember them with great pride. And I'm very happy about those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends-- on the street where you lived, was it, was it a-- mainly Jewish people who lived on the street or it was next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were many Jewish families in the street. And as a matter of fact, there was also a young girl who went to my class. But there were other people as well, Christian people. And-- but I didn't know the children that well in the street, except the ones I, I went to the elementary school with. And of course, during the high holy days, we had a-- we had a ball actually, because in the temple's garden, we were playing around. And sometimes the old members of the temple came out and shushed us, you know that we should-- that we are making too much noise and we are distracting them from their prayers. And these were the times. But I didn't have too many non-Jewish friends in those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was bar mitzvahed in, in Szolnok, in the conservative temple, which now serves as a museum and has beautiful stained glass windows. And it was really one of the most beautiful synagogues in Budapest. As a matter of fact, that street where the synagogue stands, there were three other churches, and it kind of symbolized the common faith among all those churches, which of course, did not happen to be true. The Roman Catholic church was just a little bit further up from the synagogue, and there was the Lutheran church. And there was also the Calvinist church, named from John Calvin. And everything seemed pretty peaceful in those years, obviously. As I mentioned to you, my mother's uncle and his wife, they went together, and she-- with their dog. And he would escort her to the Roman Catholic church. He would stand in front of the church and wait for her to finish up. And then they walked right home, and the same thing happened on the Jewish holiday. She went with him. He went into the temple. She waited for him. And that's how-- that's how they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Solti, you said that those were wonderful years, late '30s, you know, 1937, '38. When Hitler came to power in '33, you were only about three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you started seeing a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started seeing the change as soon as I finished the elementary school, which was in 1940. It was time for me to go to what is called the gymnasium. A gymnasium is a high school preparatory to college. It's a college preparation course, mainly with Latin, and perhaps French or German, some other language, and heavy science. And I couldn't get into the local, to, to the-- to the state gymnasium, simply because there was a law passed, obviously by those parties, the right-wing parties. And the law said that-- it was called Numerus Clausus, which means a closed number. And that meant that you could not get, you couldn't take in more people to the first year class than there were Jewish people in front of you in the prior class.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since there was nobody in the prior year, I was not accepted. So my father furiously looked around to see what could be done so I would be enrolled in a gymnasium, so to speak. So he found a school, a private Calvinist school, which was named from the-- Hungary's regent, Admiral Horthy, spelled H-O-R-T-H-Y.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was not in the city of Szolnok. That was like, 35 kilometers or 50 kilometers east from Szolnok. So he enrolled me over there, and I had to go once a year, sitting in the classes for like, a week. And then around June, I went in with my tutor, who was my first cousin, and passed all of the written and oral tests. And at that time, I started noticing, of course-- not only from the Numerus Clausus, but also from the atmosphere of the street, how things have changed. Thugs started to approach me in the street. They started to start fights, started trouble on the streets. Also on the ice skating rink, they, they would purposely block me so I would fall. And they started laughing. They thought it was hilarious. And not only with me, but with other Jewish children, they did the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they call you names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called you names, dirty names, and dirty Jew, what have you. They always made fun of your nose, you know. You have a Jewish nose, or what have you. You were afraid to-- pretty much to show up on the street, because of these thugs, who were perhaps, belong to parties. They were like youth parties, which were-- in Hungary, they formed at that time the Hungarian Arrow Cross Party. And there was, of course, a youth movement, which, you know, was a segment of this party. This Arrow Cross Party was headed by a guy by the name of Szalasi, S-Z-A-L-A-S-I, Ferenc, Franz, who was later on executed for his war crimes. He was one of the biggest Nazis who took power. After war, he was pretty much captured and put under house arraignment, or house prison. But that, that comes in later years. So I'm just jumping a little bit ahead of myself. But things started to turn around. And as I said, in-- I had to-- the normal thing was for me to, to study during the day, and then my tutor came, who was first cousin. His name was Farkas, Sandor, who was a lawyer. And he was the law clerk to my father, and worked out of his office as well. And we went over certain material. And then after he would leave, and he would give me material from certain subjects to prepare for the next time when he came. And then I had my piano lessons, and you know, practice for, for my piano lesson. And the routine was that my father normally came home from work, from court. In the afternoon, he would see his clients, and close his office, because his office and our home was all together in one place. He would close his office at 6 PM. And then we would have dinner. And my father and mother and myself would go out on a walk, on a leisurely-paced walk, and then we would go to the park. And I forgot to mention that there was a nice river called Tiza, T-I-Z-A River, by Szolnok. And there was a nice park by the river, so we would walk there. And everybody knew that the Soltis were on a stroll, on a leisurely stroll, in the evening hours. And this was a leisurely time for us. But after the 1940s, I noticed that my parents started to close themselves in. They were whispering among themselves. They were listening to the BBC, the British Broadcasting, n, in German. And they were not open and frank about what's happening in the world around us. The Hungarian Jews, we are pretty, well, I don't know if this is the word, naive. They believed very much in it that they, they are somewhat better then perhaps the Polish or the, or the Russian Jews, by virtue of their assimilation to the general Hungarian public. They acted out and they, they worked like everybody else. They had their businesses, like professions, and their stores. And there was really not that much of a discrimination before 1940. And-- like, not as I know of. And later on, it has all changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me stop you a minute. When, when you were being sort of beat up by these thugs, did you ever ask your parents, what's happening? Why, why are these kids behaving this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course, my parents explained it to me, that things are not going well for the Jews. And I'm not sure if they talked too much about Hitler's rise to power. But they just-- they were very protective of me. And they said well, stay away from them, you know? Don't cause any problem. Don't go where these guys are. Stay away from the ice skating rink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so then we started to, to just do in-house plays. And we got together with friends and played Monopoly and indoor games. And we would not go out to the streets. And we were afraid pretty much for our lives from that point on. This was from 1940 to about 1944. Now, when it came to 1944, I prepared for my fourth year in gymnasium, in the high school, and was ready to, to go. But of course, we could not go anymore, because-- and this is the big event what I wanted to talk to you about. March 19, 1944, came. And March, 1944 was the time when Hitler sent in his troops to Hungary, because he did not believe in Admiral Horthy, the regent of Hungary. And he wanted to run the-- Hungary with an iron fist.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know about-- now you're 14 years old. Were you aware of Adolf Hitler now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I was. Yes, I was. But still, The Hungarian Jews lived a little bit in a dream world, and even like in their prayers. We had a rabbi, Dr. Heves, H-E-V-E-S Cornell, who was our chief rabbi. Every Saturday morning, I remember in his prayer he asked God to help the regent of Hungary. You know, they believed in the regent. They believed in Horthy. They believed that he can stop the, the vicious attacks on the Jews. And they couldn't visualize that one day Hitler will march in with his troops, on March 19. And we were watching this event to occur behind closed shades, and just looked out, and we were dumbfounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's happening here? What's happening to Horthy? Did he give up? Did they capture him? Evidently, they put him under house arrest. And they, from that point on-- we had to wear the yellow Star of David. But did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we could go only go to, to street in a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of what was happening to Jews in Europe, in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Austria and Germany? You, you had no information?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We did not. Now, could be that my father perhaps had. One thing I know for sure is that he had friends and-- who were also his clients, and they had a glass and porcelain store, a large store in Szolnok. And the man's brother lived in Quito, Ecuador. And these friends of ours left. And apparently, it was the last ship which took them over from Europe to, to America. And before they left, he pleaded with my father. He said, Elemer, let's, let's go. Make the move. And my father didn't want to go. Whether it was just a fear to start a new life in a strange, foreign land, not knowing the language, which was Spanish, of course. Or simply because he believed that things can turn around and what things would happened to the Polish, and Austrian or German Jews, will not happen with the Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a lot of non-Jewish clients?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had. He had lots of people who were non-Jewish people, who had their own farms, farm lands. And of course, he had a lot of people from Szolnok who had their stores. And he did contracts. He did everything in the world. So he was a generalist, so to speak. He was not particularly just on-- doing one type of law versus another type.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change the tape.
